coredata.dc:title: Comparing experimental designs for parameterizing semi-empirical and deep learning-based lithium-ion battery aging models

Lienkamp, Markus
coredata.dc:description: Design of Experiment (DOE) methods can be applied to optimize test plans of cycle life aging studies with the aim to efficiently parameterize lithium-ion battery aging models. Since different DOEs exist and their effect on the prediction performance of battery aging models has not yet been investigated, we conducted a cycle life aging study with six commonly used DOEs (One-factor-at-a-time, Taguchi, Box–Behnken, Central Composite, Full Factorial, and D-optimal) and compare their influence on the prediction performance of a semi-empirical and a deep learning-based battery aging model. The results show that the semi-empirical model benefits the most from statistically optimized test plans. Compared to randomly selecting test plans, applying DOE methods helps to consistently achieve one of the lowest possible prediction errors for a given number of test points. Furthermore, it is shown that a D-optimal test plan and the test plans obtained from response surface methods (Box–Behnken and Central Composite) require only half as many test points as a Full Factorial test design, but still result in semi-empirical models with a high prediction accuracy that is similar to the Full Factorial test design. In contrast, deep learning-based battery aging models benefit significantly less from statistically optimized test plans. The highest prediction accuracy is achieved by the Full Factorial test plan and all other DOEs result in higher prediction errors and are even outperformed by several randomly defined test plans. Instead of using static designs, deep-learning-based models profit from a dynamic test optimization, which reduces the number of tested batteries during cycle life testing based on their information gain. We demonstrate that with our proposed dynamic test reduction algorithm, which analyzes the information gain based on aging features extracted after 100 EFC of cycling, up to 50% of all tested batteries of a Full Factorial test plan can be excluded from the cycle life study without deteriorating the prediction accuracy of the resulting deep learning-based battery aging model.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Overview of the general testing workflow for parameterizing either a semi-empirical or a DL-based aging predictor. Furthermore, the checkup of the aging study performed in this work as well as three different cycling conditions are visualized.
Visualization of the test coverage of the six investigated test plans with increasing number of test points (a-f).

Visualization of the working principle of the semi-empirical aging model (a) and the DL-based aging predictor (b). The semi-empirical aging model predicts the degradation behavior based on a uniform trajectory model, whose degradation rate β is determined by the degradation rate submodel, which depends on the present stress factors. The DL-based aging predictor is an encoder–decoder with LSTM cells and two fully connected (FC) layers, which predicts the future SOH degradation based on times-series with aging features extracted during the first 100 EFC. head.ce:title: Comparing experimental designs for parameterizing semi-empirical and deep learning-based lithium-ion battery aging models

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Design of Experiment (DOE) methods can be applied to optimize test plans of cycle life aging studies with the aim to efficiently parameterize lithium-ion battery aging models. Since different DOEs exist and their effect on the prediction performance of battery aging models has not yet been investigated, we conducted a cycle life aging study with six commonly used DOEs (One-factor-at-a-time, Taguchi, Box–Behnken, Central Composite, Full Factorial, and D-optimal) and compare their influence on the prediction performance of a semi-empirical and a deep learning-based battery aging model. The results show that the semi-empirical model benefits the most from statistically optimized test plans. Compared to randomly selecting test plans, applying DOE methods helps to consistently achieve one of the lowest possible prediction errors for a given number of test points. Furthermore, it is shown that a D-optimal test plan and the test plans obtained from response surface methods (Box–Behnken and Central Composite) require only half as many test points as a Full Factorial test design, but still result in semi-empirical models with a high prediction accuracy that is similar to the Full Factorial test design. In contrast, deep learning-based battery aging models benefit significantly less from statistically optimized test plans. The highest prediction accuracy is achieved by the Full Factorial test plan and all other DOEs result in higher prediction errors and are even outperformed by several randomly defined test plans. Instead of using static designs, deep-learning-based models profit from a dynamic test optimization, which reduces the number of tested batteries during cycle life testing based on their information gain. We demonstrate that with our proposed dynamic test reduction algorithm, which analyzes the information gain based on aging features extracted after 100 EFC of cycling, up to 50% of all tested batteries of a Full Factorial test plan can be excluded from the cycle life study without deteriorating the prediction accuracy of the resulting deep learning-based battery aging model.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is published alongside this publication and can be downloaded via: https://mediatum.ub.tum.de/1745519 .

ce:section.ce:para: Battery aging models are utilized alongside all stages of the life of a lithium-ion battery (LIB) for electric vehicles (EVs). Starting with the design phase, battery aging models are used to understand the influence of stress factors on the aging behavior [1], optimize manufacturing processes [2], or optimize charging protocols [3] and thermal battery management systems [4,5]. During operation, battery aging models are utilized to predict the future degradation with the current load profiles and adjust battery usage parameters for maximizing battery lifetime [6]. Furthermore, techno-economic analysis over different use cases can be conducted based on battery aging models [7]. Multiple modeling approaches have been developed for these different use cases, with deviating accuracy and explicit modeling of the underlying physical and chemical processes [5].

In general, these approaches can be classified as model-based, data-driven, or hybrid [8]. While model-based approaches, such as electrochemical models (ECMs), offer high potential accuracy due to the explicit modeling of the underlying degradation mechanisms [5,9], their parameterization is complex and their long-term prediction accuracy can be affected by insufficient consideration of the interdependence of aging mechanisms [5,10,11]. On the contrary, data-driven battery aging models require no additional laboratory experiments for parameterization and significantly less domain knowledge. Deep learning (DL)-based approach, such as recurrent neural networks (RNNs) [6,12,13], have become popular recently due to their higher prediction accuracy over long prediction horizons and their ability to easily incorporate new filed data during the operation of an EV [5,14,15]. These techniques do not require explicit modeling of the aging behavior and, instead, are capable of learning the dependency between inputs (e.g., features extracted from incremental capacity curves) and desired outputs (e.g., degradation of state of health (SOH)) [16]. However, due to their black-box structure, they lack interpretability compared to model-based approaches [17]. Hybrid approaches, such as semi-empirical models (SEMs) aim to close this gap by simplifying the degradation processes with simple mathematical functions and modeling the capacity fade as a function of stress factors, such as temperature, charging rate, or depth-of-discharge [5,18,19]. SEMs are commonly preferred by battery engineers, as they offer robust predictions, a higher degree of interpretability, and lower parameterization effort for new batteries [14,19]. However, the prediction accuracy compared to the DL-based approaches is usually lower [19].

With the exception of the model-based approaches, the training data basis for data-based models and semi-empirical models is similar. Both require large and diverse battery aging datasets, covering the degradation of multiple batteries under various operation conditions. However, conducting these laboratory aging studies is time-consuming since LIBs are achieving increasingly longer lifetimes, resulting in test periods ranging from months to several years [11,20,21]. Furthermore, the number of tested operation conditions is usually constrained by the sparse availability of expensive test resources, such as climate chambers, battery cyclers, and trained personnel [22]. Therefore, one of the test operator’s most critical tasks is to choose the operation conditions with the highest information gain with the limited number of available test channels.

On the one hand, expert knowledge from previous conducted aging studies with similar battery types can be used to optimize the current test plan. On the other hand, Design of experiment (DOE) methods offer a more structured and generalized approach to define statistically optimized test plans [22]. Different test plans can be chosen depending on the specific use case. For example, screening designs are especially useful to efficiently screen for the main stress factors among a list of potential stress factors for lithium-ion battery aging [23,24]. Full factorial designs are helpful to investigate the influence of all stress factors and all their interactions between each other [22,25,26]. However, the number of test points increases exponentially with the number of investigated influence factors and their considered value levels [22]. For example, the full factorial test plan of Schimpe et al. [27] considers two stress factors (mean state of charge ( ∅ SOC) & temperature (T)) with each nine and six levels, resulting in 54 test conditions. A significantly lower number of test cases can be obtained by a Taguchi design, which tests the effect of multiple factors and their interactions simultaneously. Su et al. [24] use this design to investigate the influence of eight stress factors with just 18 test points. Stadler et al. [28] utilize a central composite design to parameterize a semi-empirical aging model with just 27 test conditions covering five stress factors. Also, the Box–Behnken design has been applied to reduce the number of required simulation results to parameterize a battery aging model by Xia et al. [29]. All the above-mentioned experimental designs are so-called classical DOEs. In addition, there are also optimal experimental designs (OED), which are created based on specific optimality criteria. The intent of OED is usually to maximize confidence in a given algebraic model or minimize the model’s output variance [30]. Multiple variants exist, such as I-, D- or A-optimal designs. The most commonly used design in the context of battery aging modeling is the D-optimal design [11,14,31–33], which maximizes the determinant of the information matrix and, thus, minimizes the uncertainty of the model parameters [22]. For example, Mathieu et al. [11] use a D-optimal DOE with only 17 test cases to caLlibrate a semi-empirical aging model with three factors (depth of discharge (DOD), charging rate (
C
c h
), T). Despite the widespread use of different DOE methods for efficiently parameterizing SEMs, the impact of the DOE choice on the battery aging prediction performance has not yet been analyzed.

All aforementioned DOE approaches have been used to optimize test plans with the goal to parameterize SEMs. In contrast, DOE optimized test plans are rarely used in the literature to generate training data for a DL-based battery aging model. Instead, publicly available data sets are usually used for model training and validation. Commonly used data sets are the NASA dataset [34] and the datasets published by the CALCE [35–37]. However, these datasets are small, with only up to 15 tested batteries, and have not been optimized with DOE methods. The largest and most commonly used battery aging dataset was published by Severson et al. [3] and contain 124 18650 batteries with an LFP/graphite composition that have been cycled under 72 different fast charging profiles without altering any other stress factor. This dataset has been used to train and evaluate various battery aging models [3,12,38,39]. However, this dataset size is still small compared to other domains where DL is applied, such as image or speech recognition, where thousands or millions of samples are used for model training [40]. Thus, models trained on these small battery aging datasets have a high risk of overfitting to the few samples used to train the model and, thus, losing prediction accuracy for operation conditions deviating from the conditions that have been used to train the model [5,40,41]. One potential solution to mitigate this risk is the application of DOE methods, which are already used for efficiently generating datasets for SEMs.

Instead of optimizing a test plan prior to the test execution, another approach is to dynamically reduce the number of tested batteries during the test execution based on the information gain of each test condition. In a previous publication [41], we have presented a dynamic test point reduction method that identifies a set of test conditions within a battery aging study that contains the highest information gain for training a machine learning (ML)-based battery aging predictor. Based on battery aging features that are extracted after 100 EFC, the batteries with a common degradation behavior are selected and removed from the aging study due to their low information gain for the model training. However, this dynamic DOE approach has not been tested against classical DOE approaches yet.

The main goal of this work is to understand the impact of deviating test plans designs on the prediction performance of battery aging models. Hereby, commonly used static DOE are compared against each other and against our previously presented dynamic test plan reduction approach. Furthermore, the analysis is conducted for a semi-empirical and a DL-based battery aging model to understand whether the requirements for an efficient test design differ between these different modeling approaches. In detail, the contributions are as follows:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Presentation of the conducted battery aging study, in which 42 18650 LGChem MJ1 cells (NMC/SiC) have been tested according to six different DOEs (Section 4.1).
Investigating the influence of different static DOEs on the prediction performance of an exemplary SEM (Section 4.2.1–4.2.2) and DL model (Section 4.3.1).
Analyzing the potential of the dynamic test plan reduction as an alternative to static DOEs for semi-empirical aging models (Section 4.2.3) and DL models (Section 4.3.2).
Evaluating the effectiveness of the different DOEs by comparing their results with randomly defined test plans for the SEM (Section 4.2.4) and DL model (Section 4.3.3).
Concluding recommendations for the design of experiment of cycle life aging studies with the aim to parameterize semi-empirical or DL-based battery aging models.

ce:section.ce:para:
For the aging study, the commercially available INR18650-MJ1 lithium-ion cell from LG Chem was chosen due to its state-of-the-art chemical composition (NMC-811/SiC) [42,43]. The high-energy cylindrical cell has a nominal minimum capacity of 3.35Ah and consists of an NMC-811 cathode (
Li
(
Ni
0 . 83
Co
0 . 11
Mn
0 . 07
)
O
2
) and a silicon-graphite blended anode (
S i
O
x −
C
) with 1%–5% wt Si [43,44]. For an in-depth analysis of the cell’s electro-chemical properties and chemical structure, please refer to [42,43,45].

In total, 42 cells were cycled under different operation conditions, with different temperatures, DODs, and charging rates. The exact cycling conditions are listed in Table A.5 and are explained in more detail in Section 2.4. After the initial checkup, further checkup tests are performed periodically every 5 days. The upper SOC limit during cycling was set to the voltage level linked to the desired upper SOC, which was determined during the preceding checkup. During the discharge phase, the desired DOD was accomplished by ampere-hour counting. Since testing time was limited to six months, relaxation periods between cycles are omitted to obtain higher aging rates in the given time span. The end of life (EOL) criterion, is set to 60% to capture a broad aging behavior of the battery and to increase the probability of obtaining knee-points in the aging trajectories. All measurements within this study are conducted using ACT0550 cell test systems from PEC™ and the temperature is controlled by MK 240 climate chambers from Binder™. Pt100 temperature probes were attached to the exterior surface of each cell to monitor self-heating under cycling.

The rather high checkup frequency of 5 days is required due to the demanding cycling conditions and a desired high resolution of the aging curves. In contrast to equivalent full-cycle (EFC)-based checkups, the time-based approach mitigates the risk of human errors and enables a fully automated testing approach without undesired resting periods, which could arise from not perfectly matched EFC counts across all batteries. However, this test procedure leads to a deviating number of EFC between two subsequent checkups, ranging from 28 to 60 EFC. The checkup procedure is visualized in Fig. 1. All checkups are performed at 25 °C. Initially, two standard cycles are performed to measure the available capacity, followed by a C/10 pseudo-OCV measurement and a pulse characterization at 75%, 50%, and 25% SOC with a 10s 1C charge and a discharge pulse, yielding overall six pulses. Based on the checkup, the SOH is calculated with respect to the capacity (

S O

is defined as the current internal resistance measured during a 1C discharge pulse at 50% SOC for 10 s divided by the resistance measured during the initial checkup:

The three stress factors (temperature, DOD, and charging rate) are selected due to their high expected impact on the degradation behavior according to preceding studies with a similar battery type [33,43,46]. The upper and lower test limits for each of the three stress factors are defined based on the limits given in the cell’s specification and the tested values for each stress factor are listed in Table 4. Based on the three stress factors and their given boundary conditions (see Table 1), six commonly used DOE methods are applied to derive the desired test plans. Due to limited available test resource and for comparability between all DOEs, the number of tested value levels for each of the three stress factors is set to three. The resulting test plans are briefly presented hereinafter.

The OFAT design is a basic test plan in which only one factor at a time is altered while the remaining stress factors are kept constant, which is useful to study the influence of stress factors individually [22]. With three stress factors and three tested value levels per stress factor, this design results in 7 test points, as visualized in Fig. 2 (a).

Full factorial testing involves testing all possible combinations of factors with all factor levels [22], which enables to quantify all quadratic and linear influences of each factor as well as interactions between two factors [25]. The tested FF with three factors and three levels results in 27 test runs and, thus, the most test points among all investigated test plans.

The experimental design proposed by Taguchi involves using orthogonal arrays to test pairs of factor combinations [47]. With a minimal number of experiments, the factors with the highest impact on the outcome can be determined. However, interactions between factors cannot be determined. These test designs are pre-defined, and unfortunately, there is no plan for three factors. Instead, the
L
9
design (
N = 9
) is used, which is developed for four stress factors, but it can also be used for three factors by discarding the fourth factor [48].

The Box–Behnken design belongs to the so-called response surface methodologies (RSM) and focuses on process optimization by modeling the relationship between factors and the response. With three levels per factor, a second-order polynomial can be fitted, which models linear influences, quadratic influences, and interactions between two factors [22]. The test points are located in the center and at the middle of the edges of the test space [49], as visualized in Fig. 2(c). This test plan has 13 unique test points.

Also, the central composite design belongs to the RSM. In general, it combines a screening and the OFAT design. For each factor, a one-factor-at-a-time series shows the isolated effect of one factor, while the vertices are crucial to describe interaction effects between pairwise factors [28]. The standard central composite design requires the star points to exceed the conditions of the cube, which is not feasible due to the given restrictions from the battery specification. Thus, the face-centered central composite (FCC) variant is applied in this work, where the star points are placed on the outer borders of the cube points, as can be seen in Fig. 2(e). This test design results in 15 test runs.

Besides the aforementioned classical DOE methods, also optimal experimental designs (OED) can be applied. These designs are not predefined and are derived by optimizing the allocation of test points for a given battery aging model with respect to a statistical optimality criterion [22]. In contrast to the classical DOEs, the battery aging model has to be defined prior to the test execution. In this work, the semi-empirical aging model explained in Section 3.1 is used, which is also used for all the other DOEs to avoid difference due to different modeling approaches. Among the different optimality criteria, the so called D-optimal design is a commonly used design in the context of battery aging modeling [14,22,32]. In order to obtain a D-optimal test design, an iterative search algorithm is applied, which aims to minimize the covariance of the parameter estimates of the predefined battery aging model Eq. (3). This is equivalent to maximizing the determinant
D =
|
X
T
X |
, where X is the design matrix of model terms [22]. The Software Visual-Xsel by CRGRAPH [50] was used to derive the D-optimal test plan. The resulting test plans consists of 14 test points and is visualized in Fig. 2 (d).

In order to evaluate the prediction performance of all aging models, a separate validation data set is needed with test conditions that are not included in the six aforementioned test plans. In total, nine additional test conditions have been randomly chosen, three for each temperature level (see Table A.5).

Previous studies have already shown that production-based cell-to-cell variances can have a significant influence on the resulting aging trajectories [51]. Therefore, three different operation conditions with different expected levels of degradation are selected and each of them is tested with three cells each (see Table A.5).

The overall workflow for fitting semi-empirical aging models and training DL-based aging predictors is similar, as shown in Fig. 1. After defining the required test cases, the batteries are cycled according to their defined conditions. Once the testing is concluded, the captured degradation curves are utilized to fit the respective aging models. Finally, the parameterized models are applied to predict the aging curves of unseen test cases. The differences between the two modeling approaches are explained in more detail in the following.

SEMs simplify the degradation processes and express the degradation with simple mathematical functions, which are fitted onto the experimental degradation curves. Although there are many different variations of SEMs [7], the most common approach is to define a trajectory function and a separate degradation-rate submodel. The trajectory function explains the general aging behavior of the battery across all tested operation conditions. The degradation-rate submodel defines the impact of the stress factors on the specific degradation rate for each individual battery [7]. Once these two functions are fitted, the SEM can be used to predict the degradation curves for any combination of the three tested stress (see Fig. 3 (a)).

The trajectory model is an equation that expresses the degradation of a battery as a function of the charge throughput measured in EFC. Different trajectory equations, such as linear, squared-root, or power-law functions have already been used [7]. Among these functions, the power-law is selected for this work because of its flexibility and wide application in the battery aging domain [7]. It is defined as follows:
(2)
S O H
( Q )
= 1 − β ⋅
Q
α
,
with the charge throughput Q measured in EFC, power α , and degradation rate coefficient β . While the degradation rate coefficients β can be easily optimized, the power α of the power-law model is often assumed to be stress factor independent and thus shared across the same LIBs but with different stress factors. Analogously to Mathieu et al. [11], we calculate α the average of all fitted α for each individual cell within the aging study. For the fitting, an Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) approach is used.

The second step is to link the test conditions to the degradation rate equation to interpolate or extrapolate between various test conditions. This can be done by deriving a dependency between the aging rate β and the individual testing conditions. Polynomial basis functions are commonly used to describe this dependency [4]. In this work, a 2nd order polynomial equation with linear interaction effects of the stress factors is used [4,11]:

The selected DL-based battery aging predictor is an improvement of the Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)-based Encoder-Decoder used in our previous publication [52]. This sequence-to-sequence model (see Fig. 3(b)) predicts the entire future degradation curve at once, which reduces the computation time and increases the long-term prediction accuracy in contrast to recursive approaches [6]. In contrast to SEMs, the DL-based model DOEs does not use the applied operation condition as input but time series with measurements from the initial degradation process. Thus, to generate the required input data to predict the aging behavior for a specific combination of stress factors, one battery has to be cycled under the desired operation conditions for at least 100 EFC.

The architecture of the DL-based aging predictor is an LSTM-based encoder–decoder. The former encodes the multi-model input sequences to an intermediate vector of a fixed size of 128. This encoded vector is then repeated to form the input sequence for the decoder with size 64, which are transformed by the decoder to an output sequences of size 128. Two dense layers transform the high-dimensional hidden state to the desired future capacity trajectories. In contrast to the former publication, the number of LSTM layers has been reduced from four to two for the encoder and decoder. This decrease in model complexity reduces the computational time but does not decrease the prediction accuracy. To maintain equally spaced data for the encoder–decoder, all inputs and outputs are down sampled to a frequency of 20 EFC using linear interpolation. However, the original data points are used during evaluation to maintain comparability with the semi-empirical modeling approach. Furthermore, augmentation is used to increases the number of training samples. This is done by extracting multiple samples for each battery with increasing prediction starts from 40 EFC to 300 EFC with an increment of 20 EFC and adding Gaussian noise to each sample.

The accuracy of DL-based aging predictors is influenced by the given input features. Additional features besides the SOH degradation curve benefit DL-based models and can improve their prediction performance [52]. Promising candidates for a universal application across different battery chemistries are features extracted based on the incremental capacity analysis (ICA) or the differential voltage analysis (DVA) [53]. The ICA (and DVA analogously) provides a non-destructive analysis of the battery aging and thus contains information about the underlying aging modes [54]. However, extracting the exact underlying aging modes is a difficult task. One solution is proposed by Wildfeuer et al. [55], who estimate the impact of the three major aging modes based on the displacement of half-cell differential voltage (DV) curves to fit the degraded full-cell DV curve. An OLS is utilized to calculate the required shifting and rescaling factors for each of the half-cell DVs for their superposition to align with the degraded full-cell DV. Based on these factors, the degradation modes can be estimated, namely: degradation of the negative electrode
S O
H
N E
, degradation of the positive electrode
S O
H
P E
, and loss of lithium inventory (
S O
H
L L I
). Schmitt et al. [46] have already built half-cells for the same battery type and conducted a DV measurement, for which reason their half-cell DV measurements are used in this work. All DV curves are initially smoothed with a Savitzky-Golay filter, and the fitting interval is restricted to 10% and 95% SOC, as indicated in Fig. A.11. Furthermore, a differential evolution algorithm is used, which is recursively initialized with the fitting parameters of the preceding checkup of the same battery to minimize the risk of implausible jumps in parameters over subsequent checkups. Finally, all features used by the DL-based model are the three degradation modes, the decrease of capacity (
S O
H
C
), and the increase of internal resistance (
S O
H
I R
).

The core concept of the dynamic test reduction (DTR) is that not all batteries contribute equally to the information gain during DL model training. Therefore, the subset of batteries with the lowest information gain can be excluded from physical testing without significantly affecting the model’s performance. Analogously to our previous work [41], the input features presented in Section 3.2.2 are calculated once a battery reaches 100 EFC. Each battery is then mapped into a 5-dimensional feature space based on its feature values. Batteries exhibiting similar aging behaviors positioned in close proximity, while batteries with diverging behaviors are placed far away. This characteristic is used when splitting the batteries into a set, which is absolutely necessary and which is less relevant for the model to learn all occurring degradation behavior within an aging study. This split is performed by the Kennard–Stone algorithm, which ensures similar distributions of feature values and, thus, degradation behaviors between the two datasets. Additionally, this algorithm ensures that batteries without close neighbors — those exhibiting unique or atypical aging behaviors — are included in the first set. As a result, the first set encompasses the full spectrum of aging behaviors as well as outliers, while the second set contains batteries whose behaviors closely mirror those in the first set. Since the second set provides little additional information for the DL-based aging predictor, the selected batteries can be excluded from further testing. For more details, please refer to [41].

In the following, the results of the aging study are presented in Section 4.1, followed by an analysis of the influence of the different DOEs on the prediction performance of the semi-empirical model in Section 4.2 and likewise of the DL-based aging predictors in Section 4.3.
The results of the conducted aging study are presented in this section. First, a general overview of aging behavior is given and then the influence of stress factors is examined in detail.

The degradation curves of all 42 cycled batteries are given in Fig. 4 with respect to their capacity deterioration (a) and internal resistance (b). The capacity degradation shows similar trends as the increase of internal resistance. Therefore, the subsequent analysis and the model comparison focus on the capacity-based SOH degradation.

A significant deviation in the aging behaviors between the individual tested conditions is apparent during the 160 days of cycling. While some batteries remain above 95% SOH after 1200 EFC, others exhibit a rapid capacity decrease and reach their EOL at 60% SOH before completing 200 EFC. The fast-degrading batteries have an overall progressive aging trajectory, while the long-living batteries exhibit a linear degradation pattern. Batteries with intermediate cycle lives (400–850 EFC) feature knee-points, where the degradation switches from an initial linear degradation to a more severe progressive degradation. These diverse aging trends are also reflected in the nine randomly selected test conditions, which have been cycled to validate the battery aging models. The nine batteries (orange curves in Fig. 4) cover the same spectrum of aging behaviors as the remaining tested batteries and are, therefore, suitable for testing the model’s performance for a wide range of test conditions.

The spread between batteries cycled under identical conditions increases with the cycle count and the aging progression, as can be seen in Fig. 4(c). For the least demanding of the three tested operating conditions (10 °C/10%DOD/1C), the reached
S O
H
C
varies only slightly between 97.2% and 98.1% at 360 EFC. For the second harshest test condition, the reached SOHs range between 82.9% and 86.1% after 423 EFC. The highest spread is obtained for the most challenging operation condition, where the SOH at 268 EFC differs between 45% and 69% SOH.

In Fig. 5, the
S O
H
C
degradation curves are grouped with increased temperature (from top to bottom) and with increased DOD (from left to right) to visualize the impact of the different stress factors on the degradation behavior. Additionally, the color scheme is aligned with the charging rate, where blue indicates the lowest and orange depicts the highest charging rate. Cycling a battery with small DOD results in the longest lifetime, and all 12 batteries tested under these conditions remain above 80% SOH within 160 days of testing. For these small DODs, the impact of different charging rates and temperatures is negligible. Except for the highest DOD of 36%, all batteries have a minor capacity degradation with a degressive degradation trend. The dominant degradation mode is
L A
M
N E
as shown in Fig. A.13.

Cycling with medium DODs (
40 % < D O D < 70 %
) results in a significantly higher degradation and a more pronounced effect of the other two stress factors. Hereby, the charging rate has the highest impact on the battery’s lifetime. For example, the lifetime for a battery cycled with a 55% DOD at 20 °C can be increased from 170 to 500 EFC if the charging rate is decreased from 1C to 0.66C. Regardless of the temperatures, the degradation curves progressively decline for 1C charging rates, while the curves for 0.33C decline degressively. For the medium charging rate (0.66C), the aging curve follows a rather linear pattern except for 10 °C, where the curve shows a progressive decline, most likely caused by lithium plating [56]. Higher temperatures generally benefit a longer battery life for this cell type under the tested operation conditions. For example, the lifetime of the cell cycled with 1C and 55% DOD can be doubled by increasing the temperature from 20 °C to 30 °C. Similar to the small DODs,
L A
M
N E
has the highest impact on the degradation, as can be seen in Fig. A.13, which aligns with the literature [44]. However, cell Z010, which has additionally been cycled with the highest charging rate experiences a stronger influence of
L L I
and
L A
M
P E
, which in is an indicator for lithium plating [56].

The aforementioned observations apply, even more pronounced, to cells cycled with a high DOD, which aligns with the literature [57]. One reason for this behavior is the increased volume change in electrodes with higher DODs, which results in mechanical stress and is considered an important part of cycling degradation [11]. The presence of silicon in the anode accelerates the capacity fade due to electrode cracking caused by the mechanical expansion of Si particles [58]. The impact of low temperatures is especially crucial for high DODs in combination with high charging rates. While batteries charged with 1C under 30 °C achieve 280 EFC before reaching 70% SOH, only 50 EFC are reached at 10 °C. The primary degradation modes in this DOD range vary between the different applied charging rates and temperatures, as can be seen in Fig. A.13.

First, the influence of the different DOEs on the fitted parameters of the SEM is analyzed in Section 4.2.1. Then, the resulting prediction performances are compared in Section 4.2.2 and compared against the dynamic test reduction approach in Section 4.2.3. Finally, the effectiveness of the DOEs is investigated by comparing them against test plans with randomly selected test points.

The trajectory model describes the general degradation behavior of the tested batteries. The same trajectory model (Eq. (2)) is used for all DOEs to ensure comparability. The chosen trajectory model holds the assumption that the exponent α is shared across all tested operation conditions. It is calculated as the median of all fitted α values of each individual battery. The individual values range from 0.57 to 3.03 and are visualized in Fig. A.12. Hereby, short living batteries have high exponents which indicate a self-accelerating degradation and long living batteries are assigned with lower exponents.

Among the six tested DOEs, the median values for
α
a v g
differ largely between 0.67 (FCC) and 1.26 (OFAT), as can be seen in Table 2. The OFAT design contains disproportionately many progressively aging batteries with high α values, resulting in the highest median exponent, which is
> 1
, and, thus, indicates an overall progressive degradation trend for all batteries. The remaining DOEs result in a narrower range of
α
a v g
between 0.67 to 0.84 (see Fig. A.14). The D-optimal and the FF design receive very similar values (
α
a v g
= 0 . 71
and
α
a v g
= 0 . 72
), while the Taguchi and the FCC both result in
α
a v g
= 0 . 67
, which is the most degressive aging trajectory of all tested DOEs.

The fitted parameters of the submodels are given in Table 2. Due to the design of the OFAT and Taguchi test plans, no interaction effects could be fitted for these two designs. Since all inputs have been normalized, the individual β values indicate the contribution of the respective input on the degradation rate. Among all DOEs, the term with the highest impact on the degradation rate is the linear influence of the DOD (
β
D O D
). However, the weighting of the further factors differs between the DOEs. The BB, D-optimal, and FF designs are still relatively similar in terms of the weighting of the individual factors. However, the linear influence of the temperature (
β
T
) is weighted more heavily for the D-optimal design than for the remaining two designs. The FCC deviates from the aforementioned designs for factors related to the charging rate. The Taguchi design has a significantly higher influence of temperature than the other designs. Finally, the OFAT design leads to the highest influence of the DOD on the degradation rate among all tested DOEs and also obtains a high positive squared influence of a higher charging rate on the degradation rate, which contradicts the remaining DOEs. The implications of these differences for the prediction accuracy is discussed in the following section.

The prediction performance of the SEM model fitted on the six different DOE methods is evaluated using the nine validation batteries. The predictions are visualized in Fig. 6, and the corresponding error metrics are given in Table 2. Additionally, in Fig. 7 the obtained errors for the validation batteries are visualized with respect to their relative position to the test points in the test space. On average, the MAPEs evaluated for the entire trajectories differ between 9.40% for the D-optimal designs and 14.15% for the OFAT design. However, all tested DOEs exhibit high maximum prediction errors. These large errors are primarily associated with the shortest-living batteries, which experience a sharp decline in SOH. Since the prediction error is measured for SOH > 40%, even minor horizontal shifts in these fast degrading curves result in large MAPEs.

Among all the tested DOEs, the OFAT design yields the lowest prediction accuracy. This poor performance is primarily due to the skewed distribution of degradation behaviors within the OFAT test design compared to the full set of tested batteries. This disparity is evident in the average degradation rate parameter,
α
a v g
, which is 1.26 for the OFAT design—significantly higher than the average of 0.85 across all batteries and other DOE methods. Consequently, the model tends to predict a more progressive degradation pattern, even for long-living batteries that actually exhibit linear or degressive aging behaviors. Moreover, the OFAT design does not account for interactions between stress factors, resulting in poor predictions for batteries exposed to multiple high-stress factors, such as cells Z038 and Z039. This issue is also apparent in the three-dimensional visualization of prediction errors relative to the test point distribution in Fig. 7(a). Similarly, the Taguchi design fails to adequately capture stress factor interactions, leading to significant prediction errors for batteries subjected to multiple high-stress conditions, such as cell Z039. However, the test points in the Taguchi design are distributed more closely to the validation batteries in the test space (see Fig. 7(b)), resulting in lower overall prediction errors compared to the OFAT design.

In contrast to the previous designs, the Box–Behnken (BB) design offers a more uniform coverage of stress factor combinations. However, the degradation of battery Z039 is still overestimated, with a maximum deviation in SOH of 19% at 1200 EFC. As shown in Fig. 7(c), prediction errors are minimal under less demanding operating conditions and increase with higher charging rates, lower temperatures, and greater DOD. The prediction error correlates with the distance to the nearest test point in the test space. For instance, cell Z016, which is close to its nearest test point, exhibits a low prediction error, whereas cell Z027, which is furthest from its nearest neighbor, has the highest prediction error.

The SEM trained with the FCC test design is less accurate with respect to the entire trajectories but achieves the lowest

for SOH > 80%. This is caused by its high prediction error for short-living batteries. Especially the two batteries tested with high DODs at 10 °C are predicted poorly. This deterioration is caused by the lack of test points in this region with high DOD and high C-rates at 10 °C.

The D-optimal design achieves the overall lowest

Similar to the other DOEs, variations in prediction accuracy for individual batteries stem from differences in the placement of validation batteries relative to the actual tested points. As can be seen in Fig. 7(d), prediction accuracy improves for validation points at 10 °C and high DOD, due to the inclusion of additional test points in this region. However, compared to the BB design, the absence of two nearby test points for cell Z040 in the high DOD, high C-rate, and 30 °C region results in a higher prediction error for this cell.

Surprisingly, the FF design, with the highest number of tested conditions (N = 27), does not yield the highest prediction accuracy with respect to all metrics. Only the
M A P

of the entire trajectory and

with respect to SOH > 80% are the highest among all tested DOEs. With respect to the other metrics, the FF design is surpassed by the BB, FCC and D-optimal designs, which require half as many test points as the FF design. Thus, the additional test points of the FF design compared to the statistically optimized test plans do not benefit the model training.

Although the DTR approach has originally been developed for optimizing the training of ML-based battery aging models, it’s applicability for SEMs is tested. In this regard, the DTR approach is applied exemplary on the FF test plan with a battery reduction rate of 25% and 50%. The fitted parameters and the resulting prediction errors are given on the bottom half of Table 2 and the corresponding predictions for the validation batteries are visualized in Fig. 6 (g-h). Both dynamically optimized test plans result in a higher exponent (
α > 1
) than the static DOEs. For a 25% reduction, the
M A P
E
a v g
and
M A P
E
m a x
evaluated on the entire trajectory are lower than the FCC design. However, all other metrics are worse and achieve only slightly lower errors than the OFAT design, which is with 7 batteries significantly smaller than the remaining 20 batteries with the DTR approach. Using the DTR approach to reduce 50% of all test points results in the worst prediction performance of all test plans, which is caused by large deviations in the fitted parameters from the other DOEs. Therefore, dynamically optimizing test plans is not suitable for SEMs.

So far, the test plans have only been compared against each other. However, analyzing the performance of the designs against random test selections is also important to evaluate the effectiveness of the test designs. Therefore, multiple random selections of test points with increasing numbers of test points have been sampled. 25 random test plans are sampled for each investigated number of test points. The minimal, maximal, and mean of the obtained
M A P
E
a v g
values over all random test plans are plotted in Fig. 8. While the minimum possible prediction errors remain in a similar range, the maximal prediction errors increase drastically with fewer tested conditions. Starting from 27 test points, reducing the number of test points leads to an initial small increase of the min–max spread. Reducing the number of test points below 15 increases the spread drastically. For example, at 7 test points, the spread of prediction errors amounts to 73%. However, this trend is not apparent for the test plans obtained by the investigated DOE methods. All SEMs fitted with the test plans defined by the DOE approaches result in prediction errors that are on par with the minimum found during random sampling. Especially for low numbers of test points, training an SEM benefits the most from DOE-based test plans. For example, the Taguchi design with just nine test points results in a
M A P
E
a v g
of 10.71%, which is significantly lower than the majority of random selections, which have an
M A P
E
a v g
= 32 . 02 %
and
M A P
E
m a x
= 64 . 32 %
. Thus, using DOE methods to fit an SEM is more efficient than hand-picking test conditions or using a dynamic test reduction, which results in prediction errors that are even higher than the average of random selections for low numbers of test points.

The semi-empirical aging model profits from statistically optimized test plans. Instead of manually selecting test points, which could cause a large deviation in the prediction accuracy, using DOE methods is deterministic and helps to identify test points with the highest information gain that reliably results in a fitted SEM with low prediction errors. The analysis of the prediction performance of the individual DOEs in Section 4.2.2 shows that a correlation between the prediction error for a specific operation condition and the distance to the next tested operation conditions exists. The fewer batteries with similar operation conditions have been tested, the higher the potential prediction errors get. Especially in stress factor regions that lead to high variability in degradation rates, ensuring a fine-grained test coverage is crucial to ensure low prediction errors.

However, testing all possible combinations of stress factors is not necessary to achieve high prediction accuracy scores for the SEM. Instead, statistically optimized designs such as the D-optimal design, BB or FCC can achieve a similar prediction accuracy with only half as many test points. Thus, these designs are an effective measure to reduce the test costs to fit a semi-empirical aging model without sacrificing prediction accuracy. However, the D-optimal design requires an apriori assumption about the underlying semi-empirical aging model (Eq. (3)), which has to be defined prior to the start of testing. As a solution, a generic 2nd-order polynomial equation with interactions can be defined, which is capable of approximating any measurement data with sufficient accuracy in a limited range according to Montgomery [59]. Hereby, adding interaction terms is important to model the full battery aging behavior, which is usually influenced by interaction of stress factors, such as charging rate and DOD in our experiment.

Dynamically reducing individual test points during the test execution is not beneficial for the fitting of a SEM and causes even a significant deterioration of it’s prediction accuracy, as shown in Section 4.2.3. Thus, a homogeneous distribution of aging behaviors is not beneficial for the model fitting. Instead, a uniformly coverage of the test conditions (model inputs), which is offered by the static DOE methods, results in higher prediction accuracy.

Regardless of the individual DOE, the prediction performance of the investigated SEM is not ideal, mainly because of its inability to model different degradation trends for different test conditions. The selected SEM assumes a constant α and, thus, an identical degradation curvature for all tested operation conditions, which is an oversimplification for the diverse degradation trends ranging from
α = 0 . 57
to
α = 3 . 05
. Especially, short-living batteries with a progressive aging behavior (
α > 1
) are predicted insufficiently, resulting in exponentially increasing errors with increasing cycle counts. Despite this approach being common within the literature, we can clearly see that using an SEM with just one uniform trajectory function is not recommended. One possible solution is to use multiple trajectory functions or a trajectory function whose curvature depends on stress factors [7]. Furthermore, the SEM trained in this study covers only cyclic-based aging, and does not reflect calendar aging. For realistic use cases, an additional calendaric aging study is required to fit a second SEM covering the calendaric aging behavior [4]. Also, only static operation conditions were tested in this aging study to accelerate the degradation within the limited test time. For more realistic use cases, testing dynamic operation profiles would be recommended, although it is shown that SEM trained on static conditions can be used for predicting the degradation under dynamic operation conditions [27,57,60].

Analogously to the SEM, the prediction performance of the DL-based aging predictor is evaluated for the different DOEs. For clarity, only one prediction start is considered, which is set to 100 EFC, a common prediction starting point in the literature [3,6,12],

The prediction errors for the nine validation batteries are given in Table 3 with respect to the entire trajectory (SOH > 30%) and for ground truth values with SOH > 80%. Fig. 9 visualizes the corresponding predictions in relation to the measured ground truth. Compared to the results of the SEM model, the DL-based approach yields significant lower prediction errors for all test plans. For example, training a DL-based aging predictor on the FF test plan yields an
M A P
E
a v g
of 1.77% instead of the 6.91% obtained by the SEM. This reduction is mainly attributed to the later prediction start and the higher model complexity of the DL model compared to the SEM. Due to the prediction start after 100 EFC, a substantial fraction of the degradation of the short-living batteries is already contained in the model input, which simplifies the prediction. Two batteries (Z014 and Z016) have reached their EOL before the prediction start and are therefore not considered in the evaluation of the prediction error of the DL model.

Analogously to the SEM, there is a difference in the prediction performance of DL-based models depending on which DOE was used to generate the training data. Similar to the SEM, the lowest prediction accuracy is obtained with the OFAT test design. Although the seven physically tested batteries were augmented to 108 training samples, the training data lacked sufficient diversity for the DL model to accurately learn the degradation behavior across the entire test space. The limited coverage of the test space in the OFAT design is illustrated in Fig. A.15, where the evolution of the five features for the validation batteries is compared to those of the physically tested batteries. As shown, the batteries with the most deviating aging features relative to the tested batteries are also associated with the highest prediction errors. The OFAT design fails to include batteries with EOL values between 200 and 600 EFC, leading to an underestimation of the degradation for cell Z027. Additionally, cell Z039, which exhibits an initial steep drop in performance, is inaccurately predicted to undergo a high degradation due to the absence of more batteries with similar behavior in the training set. Likewise, the degradation of cell Z026 is underestimated because the training data lacks samples with higher degradation, forcing the model to falsely extrapolate beyond its training data.

All other test plans provide sufficient data for the DL model to accurately learn the relative degradation rates of the batteries, resulting in the correct sequence of their EOL predictions. The Taguchi design (Fig. 9(b)) achieves close fits for long-living batteries and cell Z027; however, it overestimates the lifespan of short-lived batteries due to the limited number of samples exhibiting rapid degradation behavior (see Fig. A.15). The BB, D-optimal, and FCC designs produce aging predictors with higher prediction accuracy than the two aforementioned test designs. Their error metrics (see Table 3) are similar but the individual predictions show slight differences. The BB design (Fig. 9(c)) provides the closest fit for cell Z039, yet it tends to overestimate the lifespan of short-lived cells more than the other to designs. In contrast, the D-optimal design (Fig. 9(d)) yields lower prediction errors for long-lived cells but overestimates the lifespan of cell Z027 and fails to capture its knee point. The FCC design (Fig. 9(e)) produces the lowest prediction errors for short-lived cells but incorrectly predicts a linear degradation for cell Z027, fails to capture its knee point, and overestimates the lifetime of Z038. The lowest prediction errors are achieved using the FF design, which has the largest number of training samples and the most diverse aging conditions. As shown in Fig. 9(f), most batteries are predicted to closely match the actual measurements, due to the high similarity between the validation batteries and those in the training set (Fig. A.15). Notably, the knee point for cell Z039 is correctly predicted. However, the degradation of cell Z038 is still underestimated.

Alternatively to the static designs, the dynamic test reduction approach can be applied to reduce the test costs to train the DL-based battery aging model. To evaluate the prediction performance of this approach, analogously to the SEM, two exemplary scenarios with a 25% and a 50% reduction of the initial test points of the FF test design are investigated. The resulting error metrics for the nine validation batteries are given in the bottom part of Table 3 and the corresponding predictions are visualized in Fig. 9 (g-h). As can be seen, both tested scenarios result in similar error metrics, which are on par with the prediction errors obtained for the full factorial test design without any removed batteries. In 9 (g) it can be seen, that the individual predictions of the DTR variant with a test reduction of 25% results in an even closer fit between the predictions with the ground truth than the original FF design. Especially the predicted degradation of cell Z038, whose degradation is overestimated by all models trained with static DOE methods, is closely matching the actual measurements. Furthermore, the change from a linear to a more progressive degradation within cell Z027 is also correctly predicted. However, this change is not predicted at all for the DTR scenario with a 50% reduction. Instead, cell Z038 and Z039 are predicted to degrade linearly, while the remaining batteries are predicted similarly to the DTR scenario with the lower removed number of batteries. This explained the slightly higher prediction errors of the 50% scenario over the 25% scenario.

Analogously to the SEM, the efficiency of the DOEs is evaluated against random test selections. The results are visualized in Fig. 10. Analogously to the SEM, reducing the number of test points increases the prediction error averaged over all random selections and the spread between the lowest and highest prediction error. However, the DL-based modeling approach’s overall prediction errors and the spread of prediction errors are significantly lower. However, none of the static designs achieves prediction errors that are on par with the minimum found during randomly defining test plans. Instead, the resulting prediction errors are close or even above the mean of all tested test plans. For example, the Taguchi design yields an
M A P
E
a v g
of 4.21%, while the average of all random selections is
M A P
E
a v g
= 3 . 92 %
. Thus, the tested DOE methods are not ideal for selecting test conditions to efficiently train the tested LSTM-based encoder–decoder. However, the proposed DTR approach achieves lower prediction errors than all investigated static DOEs and results in prediction errors that are consistently on par with the minimum found over the randomly selected test plans. Thus, applying dynamic test plan optimizations during the test execution helps to reduce the overall test costs involved in generating the data for the DL model training, while minimizing the performance deterioration due to lower numbers of training samples.

Compared to the SEM, the DL-based model achieves significantly smaller prediction errors. On the one hand, this is caused by the higher model complexity of the chosen LSTM-based encoder–decoder, which can handle different aging behaviors for varying test conditions, as well as sudden changes and knee-points. On the other hand, the prediction does not start at BOL, but at 100 EFC, and, thus already covers a substantial path of the degradation of the fast degrading batteries. Therefore, the prediction scores of these two modeling approaches cannot be compared directly.

Nevertheless, the prediction performance of the DL-based aging predictor is affected by the choice of the DOE, analogously to the SEM. The lowest prediction accuracy is obtained by the OFAT test design, which appears to be the least favorable test design for battery aging modeling in general. The highest prediction accuracy is achieved by the FF design. Unlike the SEM, the DL-based aging predictor does not profit from the static DOE methods. None of the statistically optimized DOEs provides a seamless coverage of aging features, which is favorable for low prediction errors. Since the prediction accuracy of DL models deteriorates usually drastically once the models have to extrapolate beyond the training data [61], all extreme conditions (corners of the test space) should be tested physically to ensure that these edge cases are available for model training to prevent extrapolations. However, this is not granted by the Taguchi, BB and D-optimal design. Furthermore, it is shown that the prediction error usually increases with the dissimilarity of the aging feature trends compared to the training batteries. However, static DOEs cannot take into account these aging features, because these features are not available prior to the start of the aging study and optimizing a test plan based on the stress factors does not necessarily ensure an optimized test plan with respect to the aging features. This can only be ensured with dynamic test plan optimization approaches. One of these approaches is the proposed DTR, which requires every battery to be cycled for 100 EFC to efficiently assess the occurring degradation behavior and the relevance of each battery for the model training. With this approach, up to 50% of all batteries within the FF design can be stopped after 100 EFC and, thus, removed from the training data, without affecting the prediction performance of the resulting DL model. However, this reduction rate has to be defined manually and setting the value above 50% results in a deterioration of the prediction performance. Thus, defining the reduction rate results is a trade off between cost savings and potential deterioration of model performance, which has to be chosen individually for each aging study based on the defined requirements.

The chosen DL-based model is a pure data-based predictor. However, data-based predictors are strongly limited to the training data, as shown in this work. One solution to reduce the limitation of the prediction performance, as opposed to the training data, is to incorporate physical knowledge into the model. For example, Chun et al. [62] propose a hybrid predictor that consists of an electrochemical model and an LSTM network that estimates the electrochemical model parameters based on raw measurements of voltage, current, temperature, and SOC. The transferability of the drawn conclusions in this work on these physics-based predictors is not known due to the potentially higher robustness of the physics-based approaches.

The results presented in this study are specific to the aging behavior of the LG Chem 18650 MJ1 cell. However, the electrochemical degradation processes within LIBs are known to vary across different chemistries [63]. As a result, the universal applicability of these findings to other battery types remains uncertain. Additionally, this study focuses on three stress factors due to limited test resources, leaving out other influential factors such as discharge rates, mean SOC, and low-temperature conditions, which were not examined. Moreover, static charging and discharging rates were used in this work to facilitate semi-empirical aging modeling. In real-world applications, however, charging and discharging rates are dynamic, fluctuating over time and between drivers. Therefore, the conclusions drawn from this study may not be fully generalizable to other lithium-ion battery chemistries or to the wide range of potential stress factors experienced under different operating conditions.

Conducting cycle life aging studies to generate data for parameterizing battery aging models is both time-intensive and costly. DOE methods offer a solution to reduce testing costs by minimizing the number of required test cases. However, our findings demonstrate that the choice of DOE method affects the prediction performance of semi-empirical and DL-based battery aging models in distinct ways. Semi-empirical models benefit significantly from test plans optimized with DOE methods, as shown in a comparison against test plans with randomly selected test points. Furthermore, it is shown that conducting full factorial experiments is not necessary to achieve high model accuracy. Instead, statistically optimized test plans (D-optimal, BB, FCC) yield similar prediction errors, although they require only half as many physically tested batteries.

In contrast, the investigated DL-based aging predictor does not benefit from statistically optimized test plans. The highest prediction accuracy is achieved using the full factorial test plan, which is the largest test plan in this study. All other DOE methods result in lower prediction accuracy scores. Furthermore, these statistically optimized test plans are also outperformed by many randomly selected test plans. This discrepancy arises from the mismatch between the statistically optimized distribution of test conditions and the actual distribution of input features used by the DL-based predictor. However, this issue can be mitigated by applying a dynamic test reduction, which optimizes a test plan during the test execution by removing batteries with low information gain from an initial large test plan. It is shown for an exemplary cycle life aging study that up to 50% of the test points of a full factorial test plan can be removed without compromising the prediction accuracy of the resulting DL model. Therefore, dynamic test reduction approaches are recommended over statistically optimized test plans for an cost effective training data generation for DL-based battery aging predictors.

Future work will explore similar studies on other lithium-ion battery chemistries to evaluate the broader applicability of these findings. Additionally, it will be important to investigate how the observed trends hold or diverge in larger test plans that incorporate more than three stress factors.
